title: Entire NSF science advisory board fired by Trump administration
html_body:
What matters in science |
View this email in your browser Monday 27 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
Today we hear that the Trump administration has fired the entire NSF science advisory board without warning. Plus, we learn the going rate for a fake authorship and consider how the funding system can adapt to a surge in AI-written grant proposals.

If you enjoy this newsletter, please consider recommending it to a friend or colleague. Click here to forward it by e-mail. Thank you!
Trump has asked Congress to approve significant cuts to the budgets of the most prominent US science agencies, including the NSF, in the 2027 budget. (Briscoe Savoy for Nature)

Trump fires entire NSF advisory board

On Friday, all 22 members of the board that advises and oversees the US National Science Foundation (NSF) — a leading funder of basic science — were fired by the administration of US President Donald Trump, without explanation. The National Science Board was founded by Congress in 1950 and can officially be dissolved only by Congress. The Trump team did not respond to Nature about the reason for the firings or whether the board members would be replaced. The move fits into a pattern of the Trump administration’s approach to science advice, which is being “systematically either dissolved or eviscerated”, says astrophysicist Keivan Stassun.

Nature | 7 min read

The price of academic fraud

Researchers have collated data from more than 18,700 online advertisements selling research-paper authorships online into the largest database of its kind. The team found that the going rate for a first-author slot on a fake article was anywhere from US$57 to more than $5,600. This database could help publishers determine which journals and research topics are most likely to be targeted by paper mills — businesses that produce fake or low-quality research — says metascientist and study co-author Reese Richardson.

Nature | 5 min read

Mysterious ‘golden orb’ is anemone goo

An enigmatic blob pulled from the depths of the sea near Alaska in 2023 seems to belong to an equally mysterious marine creature. Scientists say that the ‘golden orb’ is a remnant of the dead cells that formed at the base of a giant deep-sea anemone, Relicanthus daphneae, to attach it to the rock. The creature itself was only discovered in 2014 to be in a previously unknown order of animals within the subclass Hexacorallia, which also includes anemones.

Live Science | 9 min read
 Reference: bioRxiv preprint (not peer reviewed)
A National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) expedition used a remotely operated vehicle to pull the smooth, soft object off a rock about 3.2 kilometers below the surface of the Pacific Ocean. (NOAA Fisheries)

#ScientistAtWork 2026: we want your photos

Entries are now open for Nature’s 2026 #ScientistAtWork photo competition. Capture a moment in your working day on camera for the chance to win a £500 cash prize (or local-currency equivalent) and the opportunity to see your winning image appear in print in Nature and online on nature.com.

To enter, submit a photo that conveys the essence of your research to photocompetition@nature.com.

Features & opinion

Why cosmology is more than a theory

In his latest book, science historian Helge Kragh explores how conceptual models of the Universe have evolved, from those of the ancient Greeks to today’s. Universe offers an “elegant and insightful overview of the evolution of cosmological theorizing”, writes space historian David DeVorkin in his review. But the numerous astronomers who have worked to reveal the secrets of the Universe are sidelined in favour of a philosophical focus. Kragh’s “account is lucid, coherent and captivating”, says DeVorkin. But “as the concepts grow more complex, they become less familiar and more challenging to comprehend”.

Nature | 5 min read

Grant system must adapt to AI or die

AI agents can scour a researcher’s entire body of work and everything to do with a funding panel, and generate a grant application to match, all in minutes with little oversight, note Geraint Rees and James Wilsdon. The result? “In our roles as leaders of research and innovation institutions, we’ve both heard anecdotally from the dozens of funders that we work with that the volume of grant applications they receive has risen sharply,” they write. “Meanwhile, the quality of proposals seems to have improved, making it harder to discriminate between them.” A mere ban on AI is inadequate, they argue — instead, they suggest a shift from written proposals to a more holistic view of a research team’s work.

Nature | 13 min read

Podcast

How we know what we know

Nature reporter Helen Pearson’s new book Beyond Belief tells of how evidence, rather than opinion, came to prominence in fields such as medicine and how readers can harness evidence-based decisionmaking for themselves. The book features some of the pioneers of the approach — many of whom have a rebellious streak. “They had to be willing to speak truth to power,” Pearson tells the Nature Podcast. “All of them were willing to speak up and say, ‘Hold on a minute. Why are we just following conventional wisdom?’”

Nature Podcast | 27 min listen
 Subscribe to the Nature Podcast on Apple Podcasts, Spotify or YouTube Music, or use the RSS feed.

Quote of the day

“I was working through the grief of having something disappear. But that erasure was also opening up a new space that hadn’t existed, and that became the field into which I moved.”

Peter Sacks was an academic when he accidentally deleted the book he’d been working on for seven years. Now an artist, he reflects on his data disaster through his work. (The New Yorker | 31 min read)
Read more: 11 ways to avert a data-storage disaster (Nature | 10 min read, from 2019)

Today Leif Penguinson is hiding amongst the forests, lakes and abundant outcrops of serpentinite rock in the Parco Naturale Mont Avic in Italy. Did you find the penguin? When you’re ready, here’s the answer.

Thanks for reading,
Flora Graham, senior editor, Nature Briefing
With contributions by Jacob Smith

summary.overview: 이슈는 미국 정부의 NSF(국립과학기초연구소) 자문위원회 해임, 학문적 부정행위의 경제적 피해, 해양 생물의 신비한 구조, 과학 사진 공모전, 우주론의 이론 발전, AI 기술이 연구 지원 시스템에 미치는 영향, 그리고 과학적 판단 기준에 대한 책을 다루고 있습니다.
covered_item_titles: Trump fires entire NSF advisory board
Why cosmology is more than a theory
Grant system must adapt to AI or die
How we know what we know

article_briefings.title: Trump fires entire NSF advisory board
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 트럼프 행정부가 미국 국립과학기초연구소(NSF) 자문위원회 22명 전원을 해임하며, 이는 기본 과학 연구 지원을 담당하는 기관의 운영에 큰 영향을 미쳤습니다.

article_briefings.title: The price of academic fraud
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 학문적 부정행위의 경제적 가치를 조사한 연구에서는 가짜 논문의 첫 저자 자리는 최소 57달러로 거래되고 있음을 밝혔습니다.

article_briefings.title: Mysterious ‘golden orb’ is anemone goo
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 알래스카 해양에서 발견된 '황금 구름'은 거대 해양 장어의 죽은 세포 잔해로, 생물학적 신비를 보여주는 발견입니다.

article_briefings.title: #ScientistAtWork 2026: we want your photos
article_briefings.briefing_ko: Nature의 2026년 #ScientistAtWork 사진 공모전이 시작되어, 연구자들의 일상 사진를 통해 창의적 영감을 나누고자 합니다.

article_briefings.title: Why cosmology is more than a theory
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 우주론의 발전사를 조명한 책은 고대 그리스부터 현대까지의 개념 모델 변화를 통해 우주의 이해를 재조명합니다.

article_briefings.title: Grant system must adapt to AI or die
article_briefings.briefing_ko: AI가 연구 지원 신청서를 자동 생성할 수 있어, 기존 지원 시스템의 혁신과 재정비가 시급하다는 분석입니다.

article_briefings.title: How we know what we know
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 증거 기반 의사결정의 중요성을 강조한 책은 의학 분야에서 증거의 부상 과정을 통해 독자에게 실용적 통찰을 제공합니다.
